summary:
The Fall of the Achaemenid Empire (334–330 BC) was accomplished by [[event:75|Alexander the Great]], who in just four years conquered the world's largest empire through a series of brilliant military victories, ending 200 years of Persian dominance over the Near East and inaugurating the [[event:80|Hellenistic Period]] that spread Greek culture from Egypt to India.

Alexander crossed into Asia with roughly 40,000 men against an empire commanding hundreds of thousands. His victories at the [[event:1031|Battle of Granicus]] (334 BC), [[event:1032|Battle of Issus]] (333 BC), and [[event:1035|Battle of Gaugamela]] (331 BC) systematically destroyed Persia's ability to field effective armies against his Macedonian phalanx and companion cavalry. After Gaugamela, Alexander captured the great Persian capitals — Babylon, Susa, [[event:1137|Persepolis]] (which he burned), and Ecbatana — collecting enormous treasuries that financed further campaigns. The last Achaemenid king, Darius III, was murdered by his own satrap Bessus in 330 BC as he fled Alexander's pursuit. Alexander subsequently hunted down Bessus, presenting himself as the legitimate heir to the Persian throne. The conquest was not simply military: Achaemenid administrative structures, satrapies, and local traditions were largely preserved under Greek rulers, forming the substrate of the successor Hellenistic kingdoms.
